input.prompt:
I'm building a chat messaging screen in .NET MAUI targeting .NET 10. When the
keyboard opens, it covers my text input at the bottom of the screen. In .NET 9
I used WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize but that doesn't seem to work anymore.
How do I make the input bar move up when the keyboard appears?

input.initialPrompt:
I'm building a chat messaging screen in .NET MAUI targeting .NET 10. When the
keyboard opens, it covers my text input at the bottom of the screen. In .NET 9
I used WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize but that doesn't seem to work anymore.
How do I make the input bar move up when the keyboard appears?

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about a .NET MAUI issue with keyboard handling in .NET 10. This is a known change — in .NET 9+, `WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize` was deprecated/removed because Android itself deprecated `adjustResize`. The recommended approach changed.

Let me think about what I know:

In .NET MAUI (.NET 9+), the `WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize` approach was deprecated. The modern approach for Android is to use the Android `WindowInsets` API (edge-to-edge support). In .NET 10 (which would be the next version after .NET 9), the recommended pattern is to use the `KeyboardAutoManagerScroll` or handle insets manually.
 
So the real solution here is that `KeyboardAutoManagerScroll` is the built-in behavior that handles keyboard adjustments automatically in newer MAUI versions, and for .NET 9/10 I should recommend either enabling that or using edge-to-edge mode with proper inset handling instead of relying on the deprecated `adjustResize` approach. the MAUI API that mapped to Android's `adjustResize`. If that's not working, I should recommend setting `android:windowSoftInputMode="adjustResize"` directly in the Android manifest or programmatically in `MainActivity.cs`. I'd also suggest using a `Grid` layout with the input field at the bottom inside a `ScrollView`, and potentially disconnecting `KeyboardAutoManagerScroll` if it's interfering. This is a common issue in MAUI, so I can give a practical answer without needing to look anything up.
